coredata.dc:title: Phthalocyanines and graphene quantum dots nano-systems as dual anti-cancer sensitizers for photo-sonodynamic combinatorial therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Nene, Lindokuhle Cindy
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: This work reports on the preparation of cationic thiazole phthalocyanines (Pcs) conjugates with nitrogen doped (NGQDs) and nitrogen/sulfur co-doped (NSGQDs) graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The photo-sonodynamic combinatorial therapy (PSDT) activities of the conjugates were determined in vitro using cancer cells lines. The conjugates demonstrated the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) under the treatment technique. The singlet oxygen and hydroxyl radicals were generated by the Pc-GQDs upon exposure to light ad low frequency ultrasound in PSDT. Additionally, cancer eradication was observed in vitro for the PSDT treatment using the Pc-GQDs conjugates. The ROS generation and cell eradication studies showed that the NGQDs conjugates performed better compared to the NSGQDs conjugates. The PSDT treatments were compared to the photodynamic therapy (PDT) and sonodynamic therapy (SDT) monotreatments. For the combination therapies, both the ROS generation and the cancer eradication efficiencies were increased compared to the PDT and SDT monotreatments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photodynamic therapy
Sonodynamic therapy
Cationic phthalocyanines
Cancer
Graphene quantum dots

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The A) FT-IR spectra of the NGQDs, Pcs 1 and 2, and the NGQDs conjugates and B) Raman spectra of the GQDs and Pc-GQDs conjugates.
The X-ray diffractogram for the Pc 1, Pc 2, NGQDs, 1-NGQDs and 2-NGQDs.
The EDS spectra of the Pc 1, the NGQDs and the 1-NGQDs.
A) The TEM images of the NSGQDs, 1-NSGQDs and 2-NSGQDs conjugates; and B) the DLS plots for the GQDs and Pc-GQDs conjugates in water.
The UV–vis spectra of A) Pc 1, 1-NGQDs and 1-NSGQDs and B) Pc 2, 2-NGQDs and 2-NSGQDs in DMSO, and C) NGQDs and NSGQDs in water.

Degradation of 1O2 quenchers A) DPBF in DMSO and B) ADMA in after light irradiations (670 nm ± 40 nm, 300 W, generating 2.97 × 1016 photons−1 cm−2) water in the presence of Pc 2. (Insert plots: Intensity against time plots at 413 nm for DPBF (A) and at 318 nm for ADMA (B)).

The EPR spectra for 1O2 generation after treatment with light (670 nm ± 40 nm, 300 W, generating 2.97 × 1016 photons−1 cm−2), and or ultrasound (1 MHz frequency, 1 W·cm−2, 100 % duty cycles) in the presence of TEMP (50 mM) in water.

The EPR spectra for OH generation after treatment with light (670 nm ± 40 nm, 300 W, generating 2.97 × 1016 photons−1 cm−2), and or ultrasound (1 MHz, 1 W·cm−2, 100 % duty cycles) in the presence of DMPO (50 mM) in water.

The MCF-7 cells survival 24 h post PDT (light: 670 nm ± 40 nm, 300 W, generating 2.97 × 1016 photons−1 cm−2), SDT (ultrasound: 1 MHz, 1 W·cm−2, 100 % duty cycles) or PSDT treatments in the presence of the NGQDs, NSGQDs, Pc 1 and Pc 2, and the Pcs-GQDs conjugates at varied concentrations. n = 3.

The HeLa cells survival 24 h post PDT (light: 670 nm ± 40 nm, 300 W, generating 2.97 × 1016 photons−1 cm−2), SDT (ultrasound: 1 MHz, 1 W·cm−2, 100 % duty cycles) or PSDT treatments in the presence of the NGQDs, NSGQDs, Pc 1 and Pc 2, and the Pcs-GQDs conjugates at varied concentrations. n = 3.

The micro-images of the MCF-7 and HeLa cells 24 h post PSDT treatments (light: 670 nm ± 40 nm, 300 W, generating 2.97 × 1016 photons−1 cm−2, and ultrasound: 1 MHz, 1 W·cm−2, 100 % duty cycles) in the presence of the NGQDs, NSGQDs, Pc 1 and Pc 2, and the Pcs-GQDs conjugates at varied concentrations. n = 3.
Cellular uptake of the Pcs and conjugates 24 h after exposure at varied concentrations. n = 3.
Preparation of Pc-GQDs through π-π conjugation.
Summary of the structural characteristics of the NGQDs, NSGQDs and the Pc-GQDs conjugates.
Summary of the photophysical and photochemical properties of the Pcs, GQDs and Pc-GQDs conjugates in DMSO.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Values in brackets are for in water.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of the EPR signal intensities for 1O2 and OH generation using TEMP and DMPO in water.
Summary of the cell survival percentages after PDT, SDT and PSDT treatments with the Pcs and Pc-GQDs conjugate 50 μg/mL concentration.
Comparison of the DLS sizes, singlet oxygen and hydroxide radical generating abilities and IC50 for the Pc complexes 1 and 2 in the presence of GQDs and Au/Ag NPs.

head.ce:title: Phthalocyanines and graphene quantum dots nano-systems as dual anti-cancer sensitizers for photo-sonodynamic combinatorial therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanotechnology Innovation, P.O. 94, Rhodes University, Makhanda, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nanotechnology Innovation, P.O. 94, Rhodes University, Makhanda, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports on the preparation of cationic thiazole phthalocyanines (Pcs) conjugates with nitrogen doped (NGQDs) and nitrogen/sulfur co-doped (NSGQDs) graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The photo-sonodynamic combinatorial therapy (PSDT) activities of the conjugates were determined in vitro using cancer cells lines. The conjugates demonstrated the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) under the treatment technique. The singlet oxygen and hydroxyl radicals were generated by the Pc-GQDs upon exposure to light ad low frequency ultrasound in PSDT. Additionally, cancer eradication was observed in vitro for the PSDT treatment using the Pc-GQDs conjugates. The ROS generation and cell eradication studies showed that the NGQDs conjugates performed better compared to the NSGQDs conjugates. The PSDT treatments were compared to the photodynamic therapy (PDT) and sonodynamic therapy (SDT) monotreatments. For the combination therapies, both the ROS generation and the cancer eradication efficiencies were increased compared to the PDT and SDT monotreatments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photo-sonodynamic therapy (PSDT) is an emerging technique in the development of anticancer therapeutics [1–3]. The technique utilizes the combination of light and ultrasound in the presence of a non-toxic chemical compound, known as a sensitizer, to elicit anticancer effects [4,5]. The therapeutic technique where light alone is used for sensitizer activation is known as photodynamic therapy (PDT) [6] and for ultrasound alone it is known as sonodynamic therapy (SDT) [7]. In PSDT, ultrasound is employed to supplement the light irradiation and address the limitation of light penetrability in PDT. The light penetrability is limited to 10 mm past the epidermis [8,9]. This makes it harder to eradicate deep seated tumors using the light-dependent technique. The ultrasound, however, has improved penetrability past the epidermis compared to light [9–11], thus, making it possible for the eradication of deep-seated tumors using a minimally invasive and controllable technique. Since the ultrasound is used in combination with light in PSDT, a single sensitizer type that may efficiently be activated by both stimuli is more favourable. Several sensitizers have been reported for PSDT including porphyrins, corroles, and phthalocyanines, to mention a few [12–15]. In this work, phthalocyanines (Pcs) were studied for PSDT. The use of Pcs in PSDT has been reported in the literature to a lesser extent compared to PDT. The mechanism of anticancer action in PSDT has been reported to involve the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [16,17]. The ROS thereafter induce cytotoxicity through oxidative stress [7,18]. Pcs exhibit efficient ROS generation and anticancer activity in PSDT [19,20]. In the current work, the cationic thiazole substituted Pc derivatives; (2(3)-[tetra-(methyl-2-mercapto-4-methyl-5-thiazoleaceticacid) zinc(II)) Pc 1 and (2,(3)-[tetra-(ethyl-2-mercapto-4-methyl-5-thiazoleaceticacid) zinc(II)) Pc 2 (Scheme 1 ) were studied as sensitizers for the treatment of cancer cells in vitro. Cations are favourable in cancer therapeutics. Cations may be used for the purpose of targeting cells through electrostatic attraction to the anionic cancer cell membrane [21–23]. The effect of the chain length of the quaternizing agent will be examined. A bulky substituent was chosen to reduce aggregation. Pcs are usually conjugated to nanomaterials which are used mainly as delivery agents. The nanoparticles include inorganic metallic nanoparticles such as gold or silver nanoparticles or carbon-based nanoparticles [24,25]. NPs may improve the targeting of the Pcs to tumor sites through a phenomenon known as the enhanced permeation retention effect.

In this work, we employ graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The GQDs nanoparticles are highly fluorescent hydrophilic carbon-based flat carbon sheets with a conjugated π-electron rich system [26]. The π-electron rich aromatic sheets may be loaded with Pc molecules through π-π interactions [27]. The GQDs are easily modifiable to improve their therapeutic profiles. Heteroatoms, such as N and S have been introduced within GQDs [28]. The doping of GQDs has been reported to improve photoactivity [28]. In this work, the N-doped (NGQDs) and NS-co doped (NSGQDs) GQDs were studied. We study Pcs in the presence of the NGQDs and NSQGDs for PSDT for the first time. We compare the PSDT behavior of the Pc in the presence of GQDS with those in the presence of the reported metallic (Au/Ag) nanoparticles [29].
Details of the instruments used in this work are given in the Supplementary Information.

The reagents and solvents used in this work were reagent grade and purchased from commercial suppliers. The 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), anthracene-9,10-diyl-bis-methylmalonate (ADMA), 2,2,6,6-tetra-methylpiperidone (TEMP), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO), dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), and the unsubstituted zinc Pc (ZnPc) were all purchased from Sigma Aldrich®, United States of America. Ultra-pure water was obtained from ELGA, Veolia water PURELAB, flex system, Marlow, United Kingdom. The human breast adenocarcinoma Michigan cancer-7 (MCF-7) cell line were purchased from Cellonex®, South Africa. The cervical cancer Henrietta Lacks (HeLa) cell line were purchased from ATCC®, United States of America. The Dulbecco phosphate-buffer saline (DPBS) and Dulbecco's modified eagle's medium (DMEM) with and without phenyl-red were purchased from Lonza®, Switzerland. The heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS); the 100 μg/mL-penicillin: 100 unit/mL-streptomycin-amphotericin-B antibiotic mixture (PSA) and neutral red cell proliferation reagent (WST-1) were all purchased from Biowest®, France. The syntheses of the quaternized cationic thiazole substituted Pc 1 and Pc 2 [29] and of NGQDs and NSGQDs have been reported [30], and are briefly discussed in the Supporting Information.

To prepare the Pc-GQDs conjugates, separate solutions containing 5 mg of each of the NGQDs and NSGQDs were prepared in 2 mL of 1:5 v/v water: DMF, and the mixtures were sonicated for 30 min. Separate solutions containing 5 mg each of the Pcs (Pc 1 or Pc 2) were also prepared in 2 mL of DMF. To each of the Pc solutions, the NGQDs and NSGQDs solutions were added to make four separate mixtures. The Pc-GQDs mixtures were sonicated for 4 h and thereafter left to stir at room temperature for 48 h. The conjugates were precipitated using ethanol to give 1-NGQDs, 1-NSGQDs, 2-NGQDs and 2-NSGQDs.

The structures of the Pcs-GQDs conjugates were characterized using Fourier-Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Raman spectroscopy, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) spectroscopy.

The photophysical properties for the Pcs, GQDs and Pc-GQDs conjugates were studied using comparative methods as described in the literature [31]. Fluorescence quantum yields (ΦF) were calculated using the unsubstituted ZnPc as a standard with Φ F of 0.20 in DMSO [32] for excitation at 610 nm (where the Pc absorbs). The triplet quantum yields (ΦT) values for the Pcs were calculated using the unsubstituted ZnPc as the standard with ΦT of 0.65 in DMSO [33].

Singlet oxygen quantum yields (ΦΔ) were calculated following methods as described in the literature [31]. Solutions containing the Pcs and quencher were prepared and irradiated for 1 min at 10 s intervals for irradiations in the presence of DPBF in DMSO and for 60 min at 10 min intervals for irradiations in the presence of ADMA in water. Irradiations were performed using the projector lamp at 670 nm ± 40 nm, 300 W, generating 2.97 × 1016 photons−1 cm−2, the details are in the Supplementary information. The ΦΔ values were calculated using the unsubstituted ZnPc as the standard with ΦΔ of 0.67 in DMSO [31] and the AlPcSmix (a mixture of dissimilarly sulfonated Pcs and synthesized as reported before [34]) was used as a standard with ΦΔ of 0.34 in water [35].

The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was further used to determine ROS generation for the Pcs under light, ultrasound (using continuous ultrasonication at 1 MHz:1 W·cm−2 frequency: power, at 100 % duty cycles) and the combination of light and ultrasound irradiations in water. The two spin trapping reagents, TEMP (for 1O2) and DMPO (for OH) were used. Solutions containing the Pc complexes and EPR spin trapping reagents were prepared and irradiated for 5 min in water. The EPR spectra were thereafter collected, and the signal peak intensities were compared.

For the in vitro cytotoxicity studies, the passage number for the HeLa cells used was at 37 and for MCF-7 cells was 41. The MCF-7 and HeLa cells were seeded into 96-well plates to ~10,000 cells/well and incubated in culture media containing (DMEM with 4.5 g/L glucose with l-glutamine and phenol red, supplemented with 10 % FBS (v/v) and 1 % PSA (v/v)) and incubated at 37 °C, with ~5 % CO2 humidified atmosphere for 24 h. The cells were treated with each of the Pcs, GQDs and Pc-GQDs conjugates at different concentrations (0–50 μg/mL) and re-incubated for another 24 h. The cells were thereafter washed with DPBS. The DMEM (without the phenyl red) was added to each well. The cells were then exposed to light, ultrasound and combination irradiations. For cell toxicity studies, the PDT was performed using the Modulight® Medical Laser System (MLS) 7710-680 channel Turnkey laser system connected to a 2.3 W channel at 680 nm with irradiation doses of 170 J·cm−2, as the light source. For the SDT studies, ultrasound irradiations were performed at 1 MHz:1 W·cm−2 frequency: power, at 100 % duty cycles. After irradiation, the cells were re-incubated for 24 h. To measure cellular toxicity, the WST-1 cell proliferation assay was used. To each well, 5 μL of the WST-1 reagent was added, and the cells were left in the incubator for 3 h. Live cells convert the WST-1 to formazan. The absorption intensity of the formazan at 450 nm was measured using the Synergy™ 2 multi-mode microplate reader BioTek to determine the percentage of live cells post-treatment using eq. S1 as detailed in the Supplementary Information.

The cellular micro-images to visualize the effect of the treatments on the cellular morphology were obtained using the Zeiss® AxioVert.A1 Fluorescence LED (FL-LED) inverted microscope connected to a Zeiss® camera.

To determine the cellular uptake trends for the complexes, the cells were cultured into 96-well plates at ~10,000 cell/well as detailed before. The cells were then exposed to the photocatalysts at the cytotoxicity test concentrations for 24 h in the incubator at 37 °C, with ~5 % CO2 humidified atmosphere. The cells were washed to remove residual photocatalysts. Then DMSO was added to each well and the cells were left to sit for ~30 min. The cells were thereafter sonicated, and the absorption intensities of the Pcs were measured at 680 nm.
The Pcs-GQDs conjugates were prepared through π-π stacking using the procedure as shown in Scheme 1.

The FT-IR spectra of the NGQDs, Pc 1 and Pc 2, and the Pcs-NGQDs conjugates are shown in the Fig. 1A as examples. The Raman spectra for the GQDs and the Pc-GQDs conjugates were also determined and are shown in the Fig. 1B.

The FT-IR spectra show the presence of the carboxylic OH and CO stretches at 3401 cm−1 and 1662 cm−1, respectively for the Pc 1, and at 3416 cm−1 and 1654 cm−1, respectively, for the Pc 2. The CH and CC stretches for the Pcs are seen at 2918 cm−1 and 956 cm−1, respectively for the Pcs 1 and at 2930 cm−1 and 1001 cm−1, respectively for Pc 2. For both Pcs, the CS stretches are seen at 744 cm−1. For the NGQDs, a combination of the stretches was seen in the spectra for 1-NGQDs and 2-NGQDs.

The GQDs surfaces comprise predominantly of sp2-hybridized CC bonds making the surfaces of the GQDs relatively flat. The characteristic Raman spectra for the GQDs are shown in the Fig. 1B, showing an intense G-band, and a relatively smaller D-band for both the GQDs. The G-bands represent the sp2-hybridized carbons, while the D-bands represent the sp3-hybridized carbons. The ratio of the D to G bands (ID:IG) were calculated and are shown in Table 1 . The ID:IG values for the NGQDs and the NSGQDs were smaller compared to the conjugates. This is also shown by the smaller D-band intensities versus the G-bands of the GQDs compared to the conjugates, Fig. 1B. Upon introduction of the Pcs on the GQDs surfaces, an increase in the D-bands is observed for all the Pc-GQDs conjugates, Fig. 1B. This is due to the presence of bulkier R-groups on the Pcs such as the carboxylic ends and thiazole groups, which increases structural defects. The Pc 2 conjugates showed higher ID:IG values. This may be attributed to the bulkier ethyl-extension compared to the methylated Pc 1. The flatness of the GQDs is reduced. Furthermore, the Raman shifting of the G and D-bands represent the stacking of the Pcs and GQDs, representing multi-layered nanostructures.
The X-ray diffractograms of the Pc 1 and Pc 2, and the NGQDs conjugates are shown in the Fig. 2 as examples.

The XRD patterns were similar for the Pc 1 and Pc 2, as shown in the Fig. 2. The sharp peaks are representative of crystalline morphologies for the Pcs. For the NGQDs, the XRD diffractograms showed a broad signal between 2θ ~ 10°–25°, Fig. 2. This XRD profile has been reported for GQDs in the literature [36], showing an amorphous nature of the NGQDs. The sharp Pc and broad NGQDs peaks are also observed in the conjugate diffractograms.
The elemental analysis for the Pcs and GQDs were performed using the EDS spectroscopy. The EDS spectra for the Pc 1, NGQDs and the 1-NGQDs are shown in the Fig. 3 as examples.

The elements on the Pc ring and substituents are seen at 0.3 keV, 0.4 keV, 0.5 keV and 2.2 keV for the C, N, O, and S atoms, respectively for the Pc 1. The peaks for the Zn atoms on the Pc 1 are seen at 1.0 keV, 8.6 keV. For the NGQDs, the C, N, O are also seen at 0.3 keV, 0.4 keV, 0.5 keV. The conjugates showed peaks of the atoms observed for the Pc 1 and the NGQDs combined, including the C, N, O, S and Zn atoms.

The morphology of the NPs and conjugates were determined using TEM. The TEM images of the NGQDs, 1-NGQDs and 2-NGQDs are shown in Fig. 4A, as examples. The size estimations for the GQDs and conjugates were determined using DLS spectroscopy. The DLS images for the GQDs and the conjugates are shown in Fig. 4B.

The GQDs appear as dots and are relatively well dispersed, Fig. 4A. Upon conjugation, the quantum dot morphology is maintained. However, the GQDs' dispersity is slightly reduced as seen for the 1-NGQDs and 2-NGQDs, Fig. 4A. The size estimations for the complexes are summarizes in Table 1.

The TEM sizes for the GQDs alone were ~8.0 nm and ~6.5 nm for NGQDs and NSGQDs, respectively. Upon conjugation, the sizes increased compared to the non-conjugated GQDs. For Pc 1 conjugates, the sizes were found to be 15.8 nm and 15.0 nm for 1-NGQDs and 1-NSGQDs, respectively. And for Pc 2 conjugates the sizes were 15.2 nm and 13.9 nm for the 2-NGQDs and 2-NSGQDs respectively, Table 1. The DLS size estimations for the conjugates were relatively larger compared to those obtained from TEM, Table 1. The DLS size estimations for the 1-NGQDs and 1-NSGQDs were estimated to be ~19.0 nm and ~18.6 nm, respectively, and for 2-NGQDs and 2-NSGQDs sizes were estimated at ~18.1 nm and ~17.3 nm, respectively. Where the DLS size estimations for the NGQDs and NSGQDs are 8.5 nm and 7.5 nm, respectively. The larger sizes are obtained with DLS technique since this technique leans towards larger particles [37]. The dispersant interference in the DLS results in differences in particle sizes when compared to TEM [37]. The loading of the Pcs on the GQDs were determined using UV–vis spectroscopy as reported in the literature [38]. The loading of the complexes to the NGQDs were found to be 1.11 mmolPc/mgconjugate and 1.06 mmolPc/mgconjugate for the Pc 1 and Pc 2, respectively. On the NSGQDs, the loading was 1.04 mmolPc/mgconjugate and 0.97 mmolPc/mgconjugate for the Pc 1 and Pc 2, respectively. The loading of Pc 1 was relatively higher than that of Pc 2 for both GQDs. This may be due to the methyl groups on the Pc 1 having reduced steric hindrance effect on the structure of the Pcs compared the ethyl groups on Pc 2, therefore allowing for increased Pc π-π stacking on the surface of the GQDs. Furthermore, the NSGQDs had lower loading efficiency compared to the NGQDs. The co-doping increases out-of-plane groups on the surface of the GQDs, hence reducing the efficiency of Pc stacking compared to the NGQDs.

The UV–vis spectra of the complexes are shown in Fig. 5 . The Q-bands for the Pc 1 and Pc 2 in DMSO were observed at 685 nm and 684 nm, respectively, Table 2 . The Q bands are broad due to aggregation, Fig. 5A and B. Aggregation is judged by the presence of two non-vibrational peaks in the Q band region [39], with the high energy band being due to the aggregate and the low energy band to the monomer. For the 1-NGQDs, the Q-band slightly blue shifted (compared to Pc 1 alone) to 682 nm, there is no significant shift for 1-NSGQDs, Table 2. For the 2-NGQDs and 2-NSGQDs (compared to Pc 2 alone), there were no significant shifts. An increase in the absorption intensities at the B-bands (300–400 nm) was observed for the conjugates compared to the Pcs alone. This increase is attributed to the presence of the GQDs which show intense absorption at the region of the B-bands for the Pcs as seen in the Fig. 5C. The photophysical properties for the Pcs and their NGQDs and NSGQDs conjugates were determined. The ΦF and ΦT values, and their corresponding lifetimes were determined and are summarized in Table 2.

The ФF values of the Pc 1 and Pc 2 were 0.06 in DMSO, Table 2. No florescence was observed for the Pcs in water. Upon conjugation the ФF values were slightly reduced for the Pcs, Table 2. The τF values decreased for the conjugates compared to those obtained for the Pcs alone, corresponding to the decrease in ФF values. The decrease in both τF and ФF values is due to the electron donating ability of GQDs. Electron donating groups are known to increase intersystem crossing in porphyrins-like complexes [40].

For the Pc 1 and Pc 2 the ФT values are 0.59 and 0.61, respectively. The conjugates showed increased ФT values compared to the Pcs alone, except for the 2-NSGQDs, which showed lower ФT values of 0.57 compared to the Pc 2 when exciting where the Pcs absorb, Table 2. For the Pc 1, the ФT values increased to 0.64 and 0.60, for 1-NGQDs and 1-NSGQDs conjugates, respectively. And for 2-NGQDs the ФT value was increased to 0.70. The τT obtained for the complexes generally increased with increasing ФT values for the conjugates compared to the corresponding Pcs, Table 2.

For the 1O2 generation studies, the changes in the UV–vis absorption intensities for the quenchers, at wavelength ~ 300–400 nm upon exposure to light irradiations at increasing time intervals in DMSO and water were recorded as shown in Fig. 6 , and the ФΔ were calculated as reported in the literature [32,41].

The ФΔ values in DMSO and in water for the complexes are summarized in the Table 2. For the Pc 1 and Pc 2, the ФΔ values in DMSO were 0.31 and 0.35, respectively. The ФΔ values generally increased for the conjugates. For 1-NGQDs and 1-NSGQDs, the ФΔ values were 0.49 and 0.43, respectively. For 2-NGQDs, and 2-NSGQDs, the ФΔ values were 0.51 and 0.48, respectively. An increase in the ФΔ for GQDs conjugates is due to improved intersystem crossing to the triplet state discussed above. In water, ФΔ values were low compared to values obtained in DMSO, Table 2. The decrease for the ФΔ in water was expected as water has been reported to quench the 1O2 [32]. In addition, the oxygen has a greater solubility in many organic solvents than in water [42].

The generation of 1O2 and OH by the complexes upon exposure to light and/or ultrasound were further determined using EPR spectroscopy. The EPR spectra for the Pc 1, 1-NGQDs and 1-NSGQDs (as examples) are shown in the Fig. 7 for 1O2 generation studies using TEMP, and Fig. 8 for OH generation studies using DMPO. The 1O2 yields were seen for all the treatments, where the OH were only seen under ultrasound and combination treatments, Figs. 7 and 8. The intensities of the signals are directly proportional to the concentration of ROS generated. Where the increase in the signal intensities from the EPR spectra indicates an increase in the amounts of ROS generated. The signal intensities were recorded and are summarized in the Table 3 .

For the Pc 1 and Pc 2 alone the 1O2 yields were higher under light compared to the ultrasound mono-treatments. For the conjugates, the 1O2 yields were higher under ultrasound monotreatments compared to the light monotreatments. Both 1O2 and OH increased in the combination treatments compared to both the light and ultrasound monotreatments. The ROS yields also increased for the conjugates compared to the Pcs alone. The NGQDs conjugates: 1-NGQDs and 2-NGQDs, showed higher OH generation compared to their 1O2 generation yields under ultrasound and combination treatments in water, Table 3. Where the opposite was observed for the NSGQDs complexes and the Pcs alone. This trend is not yet well understood. Overall, the NGQDs conjugates showed relatively higher ROS yields compared to the NSGQDs. It is believed that the larger nanoparticles enhance acoustic cavitation which results in the increase ROS yield in ultrasound mediated ROS generation [43–45]. The Table 1 shows that NGQDs have larger TEM and DLS size than NSGQDs.

The effects of the light, ultrasound and combination treatments on the MCF-7 and HeLa cell lines were studied. The cell survival percentages post PDT, SDT and PSDT treatments using the Pcs, and Pc-GQDs conjugates in vitro were calculated using the eq. 1 as shown in the Supplementary Information. The cell survival percentages for the treatments are shown in the Fig. 9 for the MCF-7 cells and Fig. 10 for the He-La cell lines in vitro. A decrease in the cell survival percentages were observed post treatments, Figs. 9 and 10.

An increase in the concentrations of the Pcs and the Pc-GQDs conjugates resulted in a decrease in the cell survival percentages as seen with reduced cell survival percentages, Figs. 9 and 10. This observation therefore suggests that the eradication efficacies for the Pcs and the Pc-GQDs increase with the increase in concentrations of the complexes.

A summary of the cell survival percentages after treatments with highest test concentration (50 μg/mL) and the IC50 values for the complexes after PDT, SDT and PSDT treatments is given in the Table 4 .

The Pc 2 showed better activity compared to the Pc 1 for both cell types under the different treatments, Table 4. This observation may be due to the increased ROS yields observed for the Pc 2 compared to the Pc 1 as seen previously in the Table 3. In addition, the extended alkyl-chain, allows for improved lipophilicity, which improves the association of the Pcs with the lipid membrane bilayer, thus improving the bioavailability of the compound for treatment. The SDT treatments were generally better compared to the PDT monotreatments except for the Pc 2, which showed higher PDT efficacy compared to the SDT treatment for MCF-7 cells only. The improved efficacy in the SDT treatments observed may be due to the involvement of different types of ROS to induce oxidative stress since both the 1O2 and OH were evident. The combination treatments in PSDT showed lower cell survival compared to the PDT and SDT monotreatments for both the cell types.

The Pc 1 and Pc 2 showed lower cell survival compared to the conjugates for the PDT treatments only for each cell type Table 4. The reduced efficacy in the conjugates may be related to the efficiency of the cellular internalization of these conjugates amongst other factors. The NGQDs conjugates showed better performance compared to the NSGQDs under all treatments for both cell types. This observation agrees with the high ROS yields observed for the NGQDs conjugates compared to the NSGQDs conjugates. Additionally, the loading of the Pcs was higher on the NGQDs compared to the NSGQDs. Therefore, more Pcs were available on the surfaces of the NGQDs for treatments and may have resulted in higher ROS yields and higher cytotoxicity effects on the cells in vitro. Furthermore, the HeLa cells showed more susceptibility to the treatments compared to the MCF-7 cells for all the treatments. The IC50 values for the Pcs were determined by extrapolating from the percentage cell survival curves, as shown in the Figs. S1 and S2 in the Supplementary information. The IC50 corresponded to the cell survival percentages for each of the complexes, where lower cell survival values showed corresponding lower IC50 values, Table 4. The IC50 values were lower for the SDT treatments compared to the PDT treatments and those for the PSDT treatments were lower than both the SDT and PDT monotreatments. These observations are corresponding with the results seen with the cell survival post-treatments.
The cellular morphology under the different treatments was determined using microscopy. The MCF-7 and HeLa cells post PSDT treatments are shown in Fig. 11 .

The untreated (control samples) showed cells with higher cell percentages for both the MCF-7 and HeLa cells Fig. 11. A fusiform shape is observed for the healthy untreated cells, these cells are also adherent and resemble a cobblestone morphology. The PSDT treatment using the Pcs and conjugates showed affected cells with a different morphology including reduced cell densities and changed cellular shapes. The cells treated with the Pcs and the conjugates under the PSDT had fewer cells compared to the untreated cells (control). The affected cells constricted appearing smaller and circular in shape. The Pc-NGQDs conjugates showed relatively more severe cell destruction compared to the Pc-NSGQDs under the PSDT treatments, Fig. 11.
The uptake of the complexes by the cells was measured after 24 h of exposure to the complexes at the test concentrations, Fig. 12 .

Both the cells showed increasing uptake of the complexes with increasing concentrations after 24 h, Fig. 12. The Pcs 1 and 2 showed higher uptake compared to the respective NGQDs and NSGQDs conjugates. This observation may be due to the higher molar concentrations of the Pcs alone compared to the conjugates since the mass concentrations are accounted for by both the Pcs and the GQDs. The Pc intensities continue to increase for the Pcs 1 and 2 for both cell types. For the conjugates, points of plateau are generally evident, these are seen at ±40 μg/mL, Fig. 12. This suggests that the Pc-GQDs conjugates begin to saturate in the cells at concentrations of ±40 μg/mL. Furthermore, the intensities of the Pcs were generally higher in the presence of NGQDs compared to the NSGQDs. This corresponds to the relatively higher loading of the Pcs on the NGQDs compared to the NSGQDs as seen before in the Table 1.

Overall, the ROS generation and in vitro anticancer activities of the Pcs when conjugated to graphitic NPs (NGQDs and NSGQDs) are compared to those reported for the Pcs when conjugated to metallic NPs (AuGSH and AgGSH) for the PSDT treatments of the MCF-7 cells as reported in the literature.

When comparing the IC50 values on the GQDs conjugates with those of the reported metallic nanoparticles, Table 5 , shows that the latter have lower IC50 values even though they exhibited relatively lower singlet oxygen and hydroxide radical generating abilities, Table 5. This observation may be due to the increased cellular uptake of the metallic NPs. The increased uptake of the metallic conjugates compared to GQDs conjugates may be attributed to the sizes of the nano-systems. Where the smaller NPS may permeate the cells more rapidly compared to the relatively larger NPs.
Therefore, more units of the Pc metallic NPs conjugates were internalized in the cells for the treatments, hence the lower IC50 values.

The Pc-GQDs conjugates were prepared through π-π conjugation to the NGQDs and NSGQDs. The ROS generation of the Pcs increase when conjugated to GQDs compared to the Pcs alone. NGQDs conjugates showed higher therapeutic efficacies on the cancer cells compared to the NSGQDs conjugates in vitro. The PSDT treatments had increased ROS yield and anticancer activities compared to the PDT and SDT treatments for the complexes. When comparing the methylated Pc 1 and the ethylated Pc 2, relatively higher ROS yields and anticancer activities were observed for the Pc 2 under the PSDT treatments. The ethyl groups improved the therapeutic efficacy of the thiazole Pcs at a relatively higher extent compared to methyl.
Lindokuhle Nene: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing of original draft, Methodology and Data curation. Tebello Nyokong: Supervision, Resources, reviewing, editing, funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Innovation (DSI) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DSI/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620), Rhodes University and DSI/Mintek Nanotechnology Innovation Centre - Sensors.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2022.109549.
